g.data-narration: Welcome to Block Cipher Design Principles. Learn the core components that make encryption secure.
A block cipher divides plaintext into fixed-size blocks and encrypts each independently using a symmetric key.
g.text: A symmetric-key cipher that encrypts data in
fixed-length blocks independently. The key is

g.data-narration: Four essential components work together: SPN for encryption structure, S-Boxes for substitution, P-Boxes for permutation, and Key Schedule for round keys.
The Substitution-Permutation Network combines alternating substitution and permutation rounds to transform plaintext into ciphertext securely.
S-Boxes are lookup tables performing substitution. They must be highly non-linear to resist cryptanalysis and are designed using mathematical functions.
P-Boxes permute or rearrange bits to spread influence across the block. This diffusion property makes it harder to deduce the original plaintext.
The Key Schedule algorithm derives multiple round keys from the master key. Each round uses a unique key for added security.
Security is the primary design goal. Block ciphers must resist differential, linear, and side-channel attacks that exploit cryptographic weaknesses.
g.text: Maximize work factor for brute-force, minimize bias in

g.data-narration: Performance and size trade-offs matter. Larger keys and blocks increase security but also computational overhead and latency.
Understanding attacks helps design better ciphers. Each attack type exploits different mathematical or implementation vulnerabilities in the cipher.
Follow best practices: design key schedules for unpredictable round keys and create S-Boxes with high non-linearity to resist cryptanalysis.
g.text: Generate highly random, unpredictable round keys
Maximize non-linearity to resist differential/linear attacks

g.data-narration: Use diffusion techniques and sufficient rounds. More rounds mean better security but reduced speed. Balance is essential for practical ciphers.
g.text: P-Boxes spread bits across entire block
More rounds = better security, but slower speed
AES: 10-14 rounds | DES: 16 rounds | Blowfish: 16 rounds

g.data-narration: Compare major block ciphers: AES offers 128-bit blocks with flexible keys, DES is outdated with 56-bit key, and Blowfish is fast with variable keys.
Block cipher design balances security, performance, key size, and block size. Follow best practices: secure key schedule, non-linear S-boxes, diffusion, and sufficient rounds.
g.text: ✓ SPN structure combines substitution (S-Boxes) and permutation (P-Boxes)
✓ Key Schedule generates round keys from master key
